value.market_read: 3-2-1 Crack: The 3-2-1 settled at $54.68/bbl, off $3.80 w/w from the prior week's near-cycle-high $58.48. The June 19 WPSR drove the pullback: a 6.09 mb crude draw to 412.13 mb supported WTI relative to products, while a 2.06 mb gasoline build and 3.06 mb distillate build signaled product supply outpacing the pull at 96.1% utilization — products softened against crude even as WTI itself fell $3.64 on the week. Watch: Whether refinery runs stay pinned near 96% into July, which would keep adding product supply and cap the blended crack. Gasoline Crack: The RBOB crack settled at $50.83/bbl, down $4.14 w/w — the weakest leg of the complex. The 2.06 mb gasoline build reported in the June 19 WPSR undercut the summer-driving narrative: with stocks rising into peak season, the gasoline crack gave back the most ground as RBOB settlements lagged a falling WTI. Watch: Whether the next two implied-demand prints confirm a genuine driving-season pull or whether builds persist and erode the gasoline premium further. Distillate Crack: The ULSD crack settled at $62.38/bbl, the strongest leg and consistent with the distillate-led regime. Despite a 3.06 mb distillate build, the diesel crack held its premium far better than gasoline — freight and trucking demand plus thin global distillate cover keep ULSD margins firm even off-season. Watch: Whether the distillate build extends; a second consecutive build would test whether the diesel-led structure can hold its premium over gasoline. Jet Crack: The jet crack settled at $34.96/bbl, the lowest-value cut in the barrel. Summer air-travel demand is supportive, but with the distillate-led regime favoring diesel yield, refiners have incentive to push the regrade toward ULSD rather than maximize jet — capping the jet crack relative to the distillate complex. Watch: Peak-summer jet demand against refiner regrade decisions; sustained diesel premium will keep jet yields suppressed. Brent–WTI Spread: Brent–WTI settled at $3.26/bbl, a narrow spread that reflects WTI's relative firmness after the 6.09 mb crude draw, even as WTI fell $3.64 w/w in absolute terms. A spread this tight weakens the US export arb on the margin — yet crude exports ran 4.67 mb/d and distillate exports 1.39 mb/d, both healthy, so barrels are still clearing, not stranded. Watch: Whether the spread stays sub-$4; further narrowing would pressure the export arb and risk domestic crude builds, and re-widening would require WTI to fall or Brent to lift.
value.chart_321: The 3-2-1 crack settled at $54.68/bbl, off the prior week's $58.48 peak but still well above the four-week trough near $43–45/bbl and clearly above the 5-yr seasonal band for late June. The trajectory from the low-$40s in early spring to high-$50s reflects a steady climb into summer; the latest $3.80 pullback is a give-back from an overextended high rather than a trend break. Driving the move: 96.1% utilization is adding product supply faster than the seasonal pull, capping the blended margin.
value.chart_gas: The gasoline crack settled at $50.83/bbl, down $4.14 w/w and the softest leg of the complex this week. The 2.06 mb gasoline build reported for the week ending June 19 undercut the Memorial-Day driving-pull narrative — stocks building into peak season is a bearish signal for gasoline-led margins. With summer-spec RBOB already in the system, the burden now falls on implied demand to validate the crack.
value.chart_dist: The distillate crack settled at $62.38/bbl, holding the top of the barrel and anchoring the distillate-led regime. Even with a 3.06 mb distillate build, the diesel crack outperformed gasoline materially — freight and trucking demand plus thin global diesel cover keep ULSD margins firm despite the off-season. The premium of distillate over gasoline cracks (roughly $11–12/bbl) is the defining feature of this market.
value.chart_jet: The jet crack settled at $34.96/bbl, the lowest-value cut and well below both gasoline and distillate. Summer air-travel demand offers seasonal support, but the distillate-led structure incentivizes refiners to skew the regrade toward ULSD rather than maximize jet output, capping jet's relative value. The gap to the ULSD crack underscores where refiner yield economics currently point.
value.chart_bw: Brent–WTI settled at $3.26/bbl, a narrow spread that already reflects WTI's relative firmness after the 6.09 mb crude draw, even with WTI down $3.64 w/w in outright terms. A spread this tight erodes the US export arb at the margin, yet crude exports at 4.67 mb/d and distillate at 1.39 mb/d show barrels are still clearing competitively. To re-widen and restore a stronger arb, WTI would need to fall further or Brent rise — the current narrow structure leans toward domestic crude retention if exports slow.
